coredata.dc:title: Carboxyl and carbonyl groups of carbon dots co-coordinated assembly with Al3+ to emission-enhanced aggregates for sensitive sensing and efficient removal

coredata.dc:description: It is very challenging to prepare carbon dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property for simultaneous sensitive sensing and efficient removal. Herein, blue-emission CDs were facilely prepared by one-step solvothermal treatment of vine tea. Optical characterizations demonstrated that AIE phenomenon of CDs came from the restricted intramolecular motion. Through selected chemical modifications for structure–property relationship analysis, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on CDs were demonstrated as co-coordination active sites to bind with Al3+ for turn-on sensing process. Fluorescence enhancement of CDs by Al3+ chelation could be attributed to synergistic mechanism of AIE and chelation-enhanced fluorescence. Thus, the prepared CDs has been used as a selective, sensitive, and effective fluorescent probe for fast Al3+ sensing (response time, 5 min; linear range, 0.5–30.0 μM; limit of detection, 0.31 μM). More interestingly, high binding affinity between CDs and Al3+ made them assembly into large aggregates via flocculation for Al3+ removal (removal efficiency, 97.5 %) with extraordinary adsorption behavior (adsorption capacity, 1316 mg/g). Furthermore, the proposed CDs were successfully applied in detecting Al3+ in real wastewater samples with acceptable recoveries (98.7–103.0 %) and superior precision (relative standard deviations, less than 3.82 %), and removing Al3+ in spiked samples with satisfactory results. The work thus gives a demonstration of the potential fabrication of CDs with AIE property, and a better understanding of sensing and removal mechanisms for more rational design of CDs with application values.

head.ce:title: Carboxyl and carbonyl groups of carbon dots co-coordinated assembly with Al3+ to emission-enhanced aggregates for sensitive sensing and efficient removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is very challenging to prepare carbon dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property for simultaneous sensitive sensing and efficient removal. Herein, blue-emission CDs were facilely prepared by one-step solvothermal treatment of vine tea. Optical characterizations demonstrated that AIE phenomenon of CDs came from the restricted intramolecular motion. Through selected chemical modifications for structure–property relationship analysis, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on CDs were demonstrated as co-coordination active sites to bind with Al3+ for turn-on sensing process. Fluorescence enhancement of CDs by Al3+ chelation could be attributed to synergistic mechanism of AIE and chelation-enhanced fluorescence. Thus, the prepared CDs has been used as a selective, sensitive, and effective fluorescent probe for fast Al3+ sensing (response time, 5 min; linear range, 0.5–30.0 μM; limit of detection, 0.31 μM). More interestingly, high binding affinity between CDs and Al3+ made them assembly into large aggregates via flocculation for Al3+ removal (removal efficiency, 97.5 %) with extraordinary adsorption behavior (adsorption capacity, 1316 mg/g). Furthermore, the proposed CDs were successfully applied in detecting Al3+ in real wastewater samples with acceptable recoveries (98.7–103.0 %) and superior precision (relative standard deviations, less than 3.82 %), and removing Al3+ in spiked samples with satisfactory results. The work thus gives a demonstration of the potential fabrication of CDs with AIE property, and a better understanding of sensing and removal mechanisms for more rational design of CDs with application values.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a fascinating class of nanocarbon particles, have recently been an emerging competitor to traditional fluorescent nanomaterials, which not only presents distinctive optical properties (e.g., outstanding thermal/photostability, tunable emission wavelength, high brightness) but also provides appealing applications in various fields (e.g., (bio)sensing, bioimaging, photoactive therapy, and photocatalysis) [1–4]. Research in the field mainly focuses on optimizing precursors and synthetic approaches, modifying structures to achieve CDs with superior optical performance, such as long-wavelength (600–800 nm) emission triggered by near-infrared excitation [5], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [6], full-color solid-state fluorescence [7], high fluorescence quantum yields to 99 % [8], narrow full widths at half maximum of emission peak to 15 nm [9], long-lived room temperature phosphorescence [10], and so forth. More importantly, some efforts have been paid to the rational use of available chemical strategies and computational simulations in understanding the structure–property relationships for CDs [11–14]. Ai et al. obtained CDs with solid-state fluorescence from blue to deep red through surface ligand modulation, and investigations revealed that intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen-bonding inside and between CDs provoked the aggregated emissions [11]. Liu et al. prepared red-emissive CDs with superoxide dismutase-like activity, and surface modification analysis indicated that surface carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups were related to catalytic mechanism [12]. Ruiz et al. found that surface hydroxyl and hydrazine groups in combination with extended sp2 network were correlated to antioxidant activities of CDs [13]. Obviously, deep insights into the relationship between structure and performance of CDs were extremely important, which would help rational designing and tuning the functional architectures of CDs to obtain attractive applications.

Metal ions detection using CDs-based fluorescence sensing systems have been considered as the extremely capable modalities because of their fast response time, high sensitivity, superior selectivity, easy operation, and on-site visual accurate detection properties [14–16]. CDs with surface hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups are normally negatively charged, which can directly bind with positively charged metal ions through electrostatic and/or coordination interactions and then cause a shift in the emission intensity or fluorescence spectrum [14,17,18]. However, multifunctional materials in combination with fluorescence detection and removal capabilities are strongly in demand [19]. CDs have been found to be capable of simultaneous detection and removal of metal ions in an environmentally friendly, efficient, and economical way, whereas up to date, few related findings have been reported [20–22]. Liu et al. prepared hydrophilic CDs by hydrothermal reaction of carrots and acrylamide, where multi-site coordination of Cu2+ with nitrogen or oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs led to the fluorescence quenching of CDs and strong flocculation of Cu2+ [20]. Ávila et al. found that CDs derived from avocado seeds can be acted as fluorescence turn-off sensor for Cu2+ selective monitoring and adsorbent for Cu2+ flocculation [21]. Yang et al. yielded CDs using glutathione and waste tobacco leaf as precursors, and the formation of Hg–S bond and the coordination of Hg2+ to the surface groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups) of CDs caused the fluorescence decrease and precipitation [22]. Among them, Z-potential, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, the occurrence of metal ion–O peak), ultraviolet (UV, new absorption peak) analysis and reasonable speculation have been always chosen to explain the linking between CDs and metal ions [20–22], and the specific functional groups are currently unclear. Obviously, rational use of available strategies in dictating the roles of surface groups of CDs in sensing and removal of metal ions should facilitate the design and synthesis of CDs with desirable properties.

Al3+, the most abundant metal in the earth's crust, is widely used in food additives, pharmaceuticals, and cookware, which enhances the risk of human being exposure to the excessive amounts of Al3+ [23]. There is mounting evidence that accumulation of Al3+ in human body can lead to neurological damage, Parkinson's disease, memory impairment, Alzheimer's disease, and so on [24,25]. Then for public health purposes, the World Health Organization recommends a maximum contamination level of 7.4 μM for Al3+ in drinking water [26]. Thus, it is crucial for environmental benefits to fabricate materials to simultaneously monitor and remove Al3+, and metal–organic frameworks [27], composites by grafting fluorophore onto Mobil Composition of Matter-41 (MCM-41) [28], kaolin nanosheets [29], or mesoporous silica [30], have achieved dual-functional platforms. Nevertheless, professional molecular design, complicated synthesis procedures, and/or use of toxic organic solvents hindered their extensive use. Till date, a few CDs using chemical materials or biomass as raw materials have been successfully prepared and developed for sensitive and selective Al3+ monitoring [31–33]. Notably, quite different from the case of the most of metal ions induced fluorescence quenching of CDs, several CDs have been found to present fluorescence turn-on effect to Al3+ with better selectivity, fewer false positive signals, and higher accuracy [32–34]. Yan et al. fabricated CDs through hydrothermal treatment of alizarin carmine and citric acid, and after introduction of Al3+, the distinct aggregation-induced red-emission enhancement for CDs was observed [33]. Mohandoss et al. selected glutathione and phosphoric acid to prepare nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus co-doped CDs, which chelated with Al3+ to enhance the fluorescence intensity of the blue emission peak, perhaps of the enhanced electron-hole recombination [32]. Although some achievements have been attained for CDs-based fluorescence enhancement detection of Al3+, AIE-based CDs for Al3+ sensing are still scare. AIE-based sensing not only avoids the problem from aggregation but also provides turn-on detection mode with less luminescence background and more sensitive and reliable results [35]. Furthermore, CDs with simultaneous detection and removal properties for Al3+ have not been reported so far, and a more detailed illustration of the structure–property relationships of CDs and Al3+ are far from understanding.

Herein, CDs with AIE characteristics were prepared by one-pot solvothermal treatment of vine tea for convenient, sensitive, selective, reliable, and fluorescence turn-on detection and removal of Al3+ (Scheme 1 ). In-depth surface oxygen-containing groups deactivation revealed that carboxyl and carbonyl groups of CDs co-coordinated with Al3+ rather than commonly considered carboxyl and hydroxyl groups to form CDs-Al3+ complex, and the high binding affinity and stable complex formation resulted in the synergistic principles of chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) and AIE for fluorescence enhancement. More interestingly, CDs exhibited good ability to flocculate Al3+ through formation of large CDs-Al3+ aggregates. Accordingly, the synthesized CDs were successfully applied for sensitive and selective detecting and efficient removing Al3+ in wastewater samples, which makes them a highly charming option towards environmental monitoring and ecological remediation from Al3+ contamination.
Details of materials and apparatus were described in Electronic Supplementary Materials.

CDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of vine tea. Briefly, vine tea (1.0 g) was powdered and mixed with ethanol (20 mL), which was then heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) at 140 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane, and a brown filtrate solution was yielded. Subsequently, the filtrate was dialyzed (500 Da) against deionized water for 24 h, filtered again by the 0.22 μm membrane, and freeze-dried to obtain CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical passivation of surface oxygen-containing groups
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical passivation of surface oxygen-containing groups of CDs was observed in Fig. 1 , which was performed according to previous reported with slight modifications [12,22]. All the reaction products were obtained after being dialyzed (500 Da), filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and free-dried.

Synthesis of CDs-PS: CDs (10 mg) were firstly dispersed in pure water (2 mL). After that, dioxane (20 mL), 1,3-propane sulfonate (PS, 2.0 g), and triethylamine (2 mL) were added and stirred at 40 °C for 24 h. The resulting CDs-PS solution was evaporated by a rotary evaporator to remove solvent, redispersed in water, and dialyzed with NaCl (0.1 M) solution for 24 h and water for 72 h.

Synthesis of CDs-PS-NaOH: CDs-PS (5 mg) were mixed with NaOH solution (0.5 M, 5 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting CDs-PS-NaOH solution was neutralized using hydrochloric acid and then dialyzed (500 Da) with water for 72 h.

Synthesis of CDs-NaBH4: CDs (20 mg) were mixed with NaBH4 solution (0.5 M, 50 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting CDs-NaBH4 solution was neutralized with hydrochloric acid and then dialyzed (500 Da) with water for 72 h.
Synthesis of CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH: Using CDs-NaBH4 (5 mg) as starting materials, the synthesis procedures for CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH were the same with those mentioned above for CDs-PS-NaOH.

For Al3+ detection, CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and different concentrations of Al3+ in aqueous solution were incubated at 30 °C for 5 min, and their fluorescence spectra were determined (excitation wavelength, 365 nm; excitation and emission slit widths, 10 nm and 20 nm). For selectivity investigation, fourteen other metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Li+, K+, Na+, 400 μM) were chosen and assayed.

For Al3+ removal, CDs (0.01–0.15 mg mL−1) and Al3+ (0–8 mM) with different concentrations were kept at room temperature for 48 h. Then flocs were separated from the solution by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The Al3+ content in the supernatant was determined by traditional inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) to calculate the removal efficiency: (C 0 − C e)/C 0, where C 0 and C e (mM) represent the concentrations of Al3+ for initial solution and supernatant after Al3+ removal, respectively.

To evaluate the performance of CDs-based assay in practical applications, wastewater samples (1 and 2) were collected from local industries (Changsha, Hunan), and different concentrations of Al3+ (1.0, 10.0, 20.0 mM) were spiked. After that, CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) were mixed with the wastewater samples, and incubated at 30 °C for 5 min for further fluorescence analysis. The ICP-AES detection for all samples was performed for comparison.

Four wastewater samples were spiked with Al3+ (5 mM) and Cd2+ (5.0 mM, sample 1–1), Ni2+ (5.0 mM, sample 1–2), Cu2+ (5.0 mM, sample 1–3), or Ba2+ (5.0 mM, sample 1–4) to evaluate the performance of the synthesized CDs to Al3+ removal. Subsequently, CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) were mixed with the spiked wastewater samples and sat quietly at room temperature for 48 h. After that, flocs were separated from the solution by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The supernatant was then analyzed by ICP-AES for removal efficiency calculation.

CDs can be synthesized by two main categories, “top-down” from larger carbon sources (e.g., carbon black, graphite powder) and “bottom-up” from smaller building blocks (e.g., chemical molecules, biomass) [36,37]. It is worth mentioning that the wide range and diversity of biomass provides plenty of precursors to fabricate CDs with unique properties and intriguing applications [37–39]. Vine tea has been reported to be rich in flavonoids, dietary fibers, and carbohydrates, and ethanol is found as the optimal solvent to extract chemical compounds of vine tea [40]. Herein, vine tea was selected as the raw material to prepare CDs by solvothermal method using ethanol as the solvent. To obtain CDs with the optimum optical property and maximum sensitivity, vine tea (1.0 g) and ethanol (20 mL) were mixed and heated in an autoclave at 140 °C for 8 h for extraction, polymerization, and the subsequent carbonization (Fig. S1).

The morphology, particle size, surface functional groups, and chemical compositions of obtained CDs were elucidated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy. As showcased in Fig. 2 a, TEM image of CDs exhibits a well-distributed spherical shape. By analysis of 100 random particles of CDs, the particle size is found to be from 4.0 to 12.0 nm with an average value at 7.5 nm (Fig. S2a). High-resolution TEM reveals the clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) interplane of graphite carbon (inset in Fig. 2a) [38]. Powder XRD pattern was chosen to confirm the crystal phase of CDs, and a broad diffraction peak centered at 20.1° exhibits the sp2 hybridization of amorphous core (Fig. S2b), similar to previous reported biomass-derived CDs [38,41]. FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 2b contains absorption bands at 3202, 2931, 1706, 1623, 1546/1462, and 1167/1023 cm−1, which could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O–H, C–H, –C = O (ketonic carbonyl), O–C = O (carboxyl), C = C, and C–O groups, indicating the sp2 and sp3 hybridized conjugated structure for CDs [12,41]. Furthermore, XPS analysis was employed to confirm the FT-IR results about the surface groups. Full scan XPS spectrum for CDs demonstrates the composition of C (70.39 %) and O (29.61 %) elements from peaks at 286.43 and 534.52 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c). High resolution XPS spectrum for C1s can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.80, 285.41, 286.83, and 287.72 eV (Fig. S3a), which could be attributed to C = C/C–C, C–O–C/C–O, –C = O, and O–C = O [38]. Likewise, for O1s spectrum (Fig. S3b), the binding energy peaks at 531.14, 532.15, and 532.85 eV real the existence of –C = O, O–C = O, and C–OH bonds [38]. Both FT-IR and XPS illustrated that the surface of prepared CDs might be surrounded by abundance of oxygen-containing groups, ensuring the good water solubility of CDs. In addition, active functional groups are easy for modification or specific binding with targets.

Similar to common organic dyestuffs at higher concentrations, CDs often suffer from aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) because of the strong and direct π–π stacking interaction or the existence of excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer among them, which considerably limits their wider applications in (bio)sensing, (bio)imaging, and lighting [36,37]. Therefore, various strategies have been fabricated to prevent CDs from aggregation, which includes immobilizing CDs uniformly in an appropriate solid matrix and coating CDs with organic or inorganic shells [2]. Excitedly, fluorescent probes with AIE characteristics will show higher signal-to-noise ratio, lower background, more remarkable photostability, and better properties [37,42]. So far, great efforts have been paid to prepare CDs with AIE features, such as modification of CDs surface with peripheral ligands to inhibit the face-to-face stacking [11], crosslinking CDs through covalent bond, ionic bond, or supramolecular interaction to induce fluorescence enhancement effects [43], and direct developing superior CDs with enhanced emission in aggregate state [42,44]. However, CDs with AIE property are still very scarce.

Biomass-derived precursors provide possibilities to synthesize CDs with AIE characteristics [42,44]. Herein, the optical properties of vine tea-derived CDs were examined. As shown in Fig. 2d, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of CDs displays a characteristic peak at 214 nm for π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, a peak at 291 nm is correspondence to n-σ* transition of C–O bond, and a shoulder peak at about 330 nm is for n-π* transition of C = O [38,42]. Furthermore, with the increase of CDs concentrations, the absorption peak at 214 nm for sp2 core redshifts because of the aggregation (Fig. S4a). CDs exhibit excitation peaks at around 225, 260 and 365 nm, which might come from the π-π* transition of sp2 core, n-σ* and n-π* transitions of functional groups [17,45]. The number of C–O groups on the CDs increase gradually with the increase of CDs concentrations, and the free functional groups will gradually decrease due to the increasing degrees of aggregation [45]. As a result, the excitation peak intensity at 260 nm for n-σ* transition increases with CDs concentrations increasing from 0.02 to 0.05 mg mL−1 and then gradually decreases (Fig. S4b). The surface oxygen-containing functional groups on the edge of CDs can form hydrogen bonds, which then pulls CDs particles closer, prompts the aggregation, and expands the π-electronic system [45,46]. Thus, the excitation peak at 365 nm for n-π* transition has a significant redshift (Fig. S4b). The maximum emission wavelength is at 450 nm under 365 nm excitation, and CDs solution presents bluish fluorescence emission under 365 nm irradiation and light yellow under daylight (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, fluorescence spectra of CDs solution were investigated under different excitation (Fig. S5). Obviously, with the continuous increase of the excitation wavelengths from 295 nm to 355 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelengths seldom change at about 448 nm, whereas the fluorescence intensities gradually enhance, exhibiting excitation-independent behavior. Subsequently, by increasing excitation wavelength from 355 nm to 415 nm, a red-shift excitation dependent manner is observed for emission wavelengths from 448 nm to 491 nm, and the fluorescence intensities are on the decline, which might come from the different particle size and uneven surface oxygen-related defects of CDs [17,38]. The absorption wavelengths and emission peaks of CDs in solvents with different polarities have been listed in Table S1, and a little influence is observed. In addition, optical stability of CDs was an important property to be studied. As described in Fig. S6, fluorescence intensities at 450 nm for CDs solution show a negligible variation in NaCl solution with concentrations from 0 to 800 mM, at temperature from 25 °C to 45 °C, under continuous 365 nm irradiation for 90 min, after 12 months storage under 4 °C and room temperature. Thus, the obtained CDs possess outstanding stability, and could be better applied in practical application prospect.

Of particular mention is that vine tea-derived CDs display AIE property, which was preliminarily investigated by collecting the fluorescence behaviors of CDs in H2O (a good solvent)/tetrahydrofuran (THF, a poor solvent) solution. With the increasing THF fractions from 0 % to 99 %, fluorescence intensities continuously enhance and fluorescence emission slightly blue-shifts (Fig. 3 a). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis indicates the larger particles of CDs in the solution with higher THF fractions (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, formation of CDs aggregates with a clustered structure in 99 % THF solution can be clearly observed by TEM (Fig. 3c). Accordingly, the relative quantum yield of CDs calculated with respect to quinine sulfate increases from 6.09 % in H2O solution to 9.43 % in 99 % THF solution (Fig. 3b). The phenomenon presents that CDs are AIE nanoparticles, and the enhanced fluorescence emission and quantum yield come from the marked aggregation of CDs in poor solvent, THF. By comparison, CDs with ACQ phenomenon was synthesized as previously reported using ascorbic acid and glycol as precursors [34], and it can be obviously observed that the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield decrease with the increasing THF fractions from 0 % to 99 %, while almost no fluorescence can be found in the solid state (Fig. S7). Meanwhile, enhanced emission with a red-shift effect is observed for higher concentrations of CDs (Fig. 3d) and CDs in solid state emit bright blue fluorescence at 455 nm (Fig. 3e), also supporting the AIE feature of vine tea-derived CDs. Interestingly, with the glycerol fraction changing from 0 % to 80 %, the fluorescence intensities increase obviously (Fig. 3f). With the increase of viscosity, the intramolecular motion (vibration and rotation) of CDs will be restricted, resulting in the closing of non-radiative attenuation channels and the significant enhancement of fluorescence emission, which is also the typical feature of AIE molecules [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive fluorescence turn-on sensing of Al3+by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, main methods for Al3+ detection were atomic absorption spectrometry and ICP-AES [48]. It was noted that fluorescence turn-on sensing will provide strong anti-interference capability and achieve more specific and sensitive results [19]. Metal ions have been reported to complex with oxygen-containing groups of CDs to realize fluorescence sensing [32–34]. Here, Al3+ (40 μM) and other fourteen common metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Li+, K+, Na+, 400 μM, 10 times that of Al3+) were separately incubated with CDs (0.1 mg mL−1), and it is clear that fluorescence intensity of CDs significantly increases only in the presence of Al3+ (Fig. 4 a). Especially, the reaction systems perform almost the same turn-on response to Al3+ in the presence of potential interfering metal ions (Fig. 4b), indicating the superior selectivity. This phenomenon might be attributed to the smaller ionic radius and higher charge density of Al3+, which allows a strong coordination reaction between Al3+ and CDs [49]. Thus, the specific recognition can be developed for Al3+ sensing with high selectivity in actual environmental samples.

To get better response of CDs to Al3+, reaction parameters were evaluated and optimized. And results indicate that the fluorescence increasing efficiency reaches the highest for Al3+ when CDs concentration, reaction pH, reaction temperature, and incubation time are set at 0.1 mg mL−1, pH 7, 30 °C, and 5 min (Fig. S8). Here, fluorescence intensity increases with the pH increase from 3 to 7, and then decreases with further increase for pH from 7 to 10, due to the hydrolysis of Al3+ and protonation/deprotonation of functional groups on CDs to render the emissive sites inactive [6,23]. Under optimal conditions, fluorescence intensity of CDs enhances gradually with the increase of Al3+ concentration (Fig. 4c). An excellent linear relationship can be found in the range from 0.5 to 30.0 μM, and the calibration curve is expressed as y = 1.1843x + 63.657 (R 2 = 0.9939) (Fig. 4d) with the corresponding limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/slope, n = 12) value at 0.31 μM. Table S2 exhibits the recently developed fluorescent nanoparticles for Al3+ detection, while CDs and metal–organic framework (MOF) are the two main types of nanomaterials. Our developed CDs-based sensing is a turn-on strategy and presents comparable sensitivity. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values for intra-day and inter-day variations are 2.57 % and 4.15 %. Furthermore, different kinds of Al3+ salts, such as AlCl3, Al2(SO4)3, AlCl3·6H2O, and KAl(SO4)2·12H2O, were also selected for sensing. As presented in Fig. S9a, types of Al3+ salts have negligible effect on sensing. After that, batch-to-batch variation is investigated for Al3+ sensing with RSD at 2.36 % (Fig. S9b). Further, sequential and alternative addition of Al3+ and EDTA four cycles result in the reversible modulation of fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. S9c), demonstrating superior reusability. Thus, the developed CDs-based Al3+ sensing strategy exhibits high reproducibility, satisfactory stability, and excellent precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction and sensing mechanism of CDs to Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The amazing fluorescence response of CDs to Al3+ motivates us to conduct deep exploration of the interaction and sensing mechanism between CDs and Al3+. When Al3+ was introduced into CDs solution, the solution changes from colorless to light yellow, a new absorption peak at 425 nm appears (Fig. 5 a), and the absorbance at 425 nm exhibits linear relationship with Al3+ concentration from 0.5 to 40.0 μM (Fig. S10a). Since Al3+ solution does not present any absorption peaks over 400 nm, the new peak might result from the formation of novel chromophore center (Fig. S10b). Based on the hard acid and base rule, Al3+, a hard acid, can spontaneously, easily, and strongly combine with oxygen-containing groups (hard bases) on the CDs [33]. Zeta potential of CDs was −13.70 mV, which increased to 0.03 mV after addition of Al3+, confirming the successful interaction of positively charged Al3+ with negatively charged CDs. Correspondingly, the full spectrum of CDs after addition of Al3+ contains three elements (i.e., C, O, and Al) (Fig. S11a), and Al2p spectrum represents Al–O typical absorption peaks at 74.90 eV (Al 2p3/2) and 74.27 eV (Al 2p1/2) (Fig. 5b). By comparing the C1s and O1s XPS spectra of CDs before and after addition of Al3+, the locations of carbonyl and carboxyl groups change obviously (Fig. S3). Compared with high-resolution O1s (531.30 and 530.31 eV for O = C–O and C = O bonds) spectrum for CDs, the binding energies in CDs-Al3+ (531.74 and 530.75 eV for O = C–O and C = O bonds) are a litter higher because Al3+ coordinates with surface oxygen-containing functional groups and decreases their electron-cloud densities. In addition, by comparison of FTIR spectrum for CDs (Fig. 2b), a new peak at 835 cm−1 appears for Al–O bond after addition of Al3+, and the C = O stretching vibration shifts to 1600 cm−1 (Fig. S11b). Thus, a new complex might be formed by strong coordination ability between carbonyl and carboxyl groups on CDs with Al3+.

To illustrate the role of surface groups in Al3+-triggered turn-on fluorescence sensing, three typical surface oxygen-containing groups (i.e., O–H, –C = O, O–C = O) have been selectively deactivated [12], and the corresponding sensing properties have been investigated in detail. Fig. 1 presents the selective deactivation procedures and Fig. 5c exhibits the FT-IR spectra of deactivation products. 1,3-Propane sulfonate (PS) has weak binding affinity to metal ions [22], which was then selected as the passivating reagent to react with surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CDs into ester and ether groups, respectively. After that, CDs-PS with carbonyl group was obtained. In FT-IR spectrum of CDs-PS, the stretching vibration of C = O for ester group at 1654 cm−1 and characteristic absorption peaks for sulfonic acid at 525, 605, 1040, and 1165 cm−1 appear, indicating the successful condensation between CDs and PS. By alkaline hydrolysis, ester can be transformed to original carboxyl group and ether bond kept stable, and then CDs-PS was hydrolyzed by NaOH to form CDs-PS-NaOH with carboxyl and carbonyl groups. The decreased absorption peaks for sulfonic acid and the reoccurrence of carboxyl stretching vibration peak at 1623 cm−1 indicate the successful preparation of CDs-PS-NaOH. Meanwhile, NaBH4 was chosen as the reducing reagent to reduce carbonyl group of CDs into alkyl hydroxyl group, and the CDs-NaBH4 with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups was produced. As a result, in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-NaBH4, carbonyl peak at 1706 cm−1 cannot be found. Similarly, after PS condensation and NaOH hydrolysis, CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH with carbonyl group was got. Subsequently, fluorescent (Fig. S12) and UV–vis spectra (Fig. S13) of CDs, CDs-PS, CDs-PS-NaOH, CDs-NaBH4, and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH after addition of Al3+ were studied. As discovered in Fig. S12 and 5d, CDs-PS exhibits the fluorescence turn-on response to Al3+, but is much lower than that of CDs, indicating that carbonyl group plays a role in Al3+-trigged fluorescence enhancement, and other groups on the surface of CDs also take part in the fluorescence enhancement behavior. The extent of fluorescence enhancing for CDs-NaBH4 to Al3+ is similar to that for CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH, much lower than that of CDs and a little higher than that of CDs-PS (Fig. 5d). The results exhibited that carboxyl group was another important structure for Al3+ sensing, whereas hydroxyl group might not cause the fluorescence enhancement of CDs to Al3+. Meaningfully, Al3+-trigged enhancing efficiency for CDs-PS-NaOH comes close to that of CDs (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, the new absorption peak at about 425 nm can also be detected for introduction of Al3+ into CDs-PS-NaOH solution (Fig. S13). Also, CDs-PS-NaOH presents fluorescence enhancement to Al3+ selectively (Fig. S14), similar phenomenon with those of CDs (Fig. 4a). These findings came into the conclusion that carboxyl and carbonyl groups of CDs co-coordinated with Al3+ to form complex for fluorescence enhancement sensing.

Coordination between CDs and Al3+ will directly influence their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels, and then control the fluorescence behavior. The energy bandgaps between HOMO and LUMO orbitals were detected and calculated as 2.22 eV and 2.11 eV for CDs and CDs-Al3+ complex (Fig. S15 and 5e), respectively. In addition, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to investigate the interaction between CDs and Al3+. Oxygen-containing graphitic fragments were selected to represent CDs, while six conjugated benzene rings (Fig. S16) and positions of oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. S17) were optimized to bring HOMO (EHOMO = −5.27 eV) and LUMO (ELUMO = −3.54) energy close to the experimental values. After coordination with Al3+, the molecular orbital diagrams are shown in Fig. S18 with EHOMO and ELUMO at −5.38 and −4.55 eV, respectively. The energy bandgap for CDs-Al3+ complex (0.83 eV) is lower than that of CDs (1.73 eV), the same phenomenon observed in the experiments. The lower bandgap and HOMO value for CDs-Al3+ complex indicated that the formed complex was more stable, which would reasonably deduce the red-shift emission (Fig. S3) and the turn-on mechanism for chelation enhancement fluorescence (CHEF) [33]. Binding energies between functional groups of CDs and Al3+ show that carboxyl group (−88.45 kcal/mol) and carbonyl group (−65.58 kcal/mol) have stronger binding ability to Al3+ than hydroxyl group (−27.97 kcal/mol) (Fig. S19). Furthermore, the higher binding constant (1.7 × 104 M−1) between CDs and Al3+ being calculated based on Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Fig. S20) also indicated their stable complexation. The average lifetime decreases from CDs (4.33 ns) to CDs-Al3+ (3.43 ns) (Fig. 5f), which then suggested that the probability of increased electron-hole recombination for fluorescence enhancement [32]. On the other hand, after introduction of Al3+ into CDs solution, the originally dispersed CDs clustered together through Al3+ coordinated assembly to form aggregates with larger average particle size (993 nm) (Fig. S21), indicating the strong aggregation. The aggregation will effectively constrain the intramolecular motion and enhanced molecular rigidity, and then lead to the significantly enhanced fluorescence, which is considered as an AIE mechanism. Thus, we proposed that the fluorescence enhancement of CDs to Al3+ was based on the synergistic effect of CHEF and AIE from carboxyl and carbonyl groups of CDs co-coordinated assembly with Al3+ (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of Al3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the feasibility of CDs-based sensing system for Al3+ analysis in real samples, two wastewater samples were analyzed, and the traditional accepted ICP-AES method was performed as the control. As illustrated in Table 1 , Al3+ concentrations in wastewater samples 1 and 2 were detected by CDs-based fluorescence method as 4.49 ± 0.08 μM and 6.02 ± 0.23 μM, respectively, less than the release limit at 7.4 μM. Furthermore, satisfactory recoveries (98.7–103.0 %) with low RSD (less than 3.82 %) were achieved by spiking different concentrations of Al3+ (i.e., 1.00, 10.00, and 20.00 μM) into the real samples, exhibiting the good accuracy and excellent repeatability of real sample analysis. In addition, no significant difference presented between ICP-AES analysis and our developed CDs-based method according to F-test (F cal < F 0.05) and t-text (t cal < t 0.05,4) examinations, indicating that CDs-based assay could be highly applicable for the precise monitoring of Al3+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of Al3+ removal via flocculation
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the sensitive and selective detection of Al3+, Al3+ removal is also vital for environmental benefits. High binding affinity between CDs and Al3+ through coordination interactions will result in charge neutralization to facilitate aggregation [22]. And at this time, CDs can be considered as “chemical glue” to bridge Al3+ together to form large and stable flocs. Finally, the coordinated flocs accelerate aggregation through their own sweeping effects, thus effectively removing Al3+ [50]. The possible flocculation mechanism has been shown in Fig. S22. A large number of yellow CDs-Al3+ flocs can be easily observed at the bottom of tubes through naked eye for CDs and Al3+ solution being stayed at room temperature for 48 h, while CDs and Al3+ solution (10 mL) with concentrations at 0.1 mg mL−1 and 5 mM are found to be the lowest concentrations for flocculation (Fig. S23). After flocculation, the transparent supernatant has almost no color and fluorescence (Fig. S23), indicating the removal of Al3+ by CDs. What is more, by adding CDs-PS, CDs-PS-NaOH, CDs-NaBH4 or CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH into Al3+ solution, flocs are found only for CDs-PS-NaOH, the same phenomenon with that of CDs (Fig. S23 and S24). Results demonstrate that carboxyl and carbonyl groups of CDs co-coordination with Al3+ to form stable complex is the key step for flocculation. Subsequently, fourteen common metal ions were selected for flocculation experiments, and as expected, only flocs can be found for Al3+ system (Fig. S25), exhibiting the high specificity for Al3+ removal. Then the removal ability of CDs to Al3+ has been examined for practical applications. Solutions (10 mL) containing CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and Al3+ (5 mM) were kept at room temperature, and Al3+ content in the supernatant was analyzed by ICP-AES. Results indicate that pH plays an important role in Al3+ removal and pH at 7 reaches the highest removal efficiency (Fig. 6 a). After being let stand for about 12 h, flocs can be found at the bottom, and the Al3+ removal efficiency reaches the highest at about 48 h, and then keeps plateau (Fig. 6b), demonstrating slow chelating kinetics. Confocal fluorescence image analysis indicates that CDs-Al3+ assembles into random aggregates (Fig. 6c). Notably, the removal efficiency is found to be 97.5 % and the adsorption capacity reaches 1316 mg g−1 (Fig. 6d), largely higher than those of reported adsorbents for Al3+ adsorption (e.g., nanofibrous membrane, 350 mg g−1 [50]; ion-imprinted polymers, 106 mg g−1 [51]) (Table S2). The types of Al3+ salts have seldom effect on the removal efficiency (Fig. S9a), and batch-to-batch variation of CDs for Al3+ removal is 1.27 % (Fig. S9b). In addition, the reusability of CDs was consulted using EDTA as the adsorbent for Al3+. After three cycles, removal efficiency can also reach 95.2 % (Fig. S26), indicative of good reusability. Finally, four spiked wastewater samples were added with CDs, and Al3+ is efficiently flocculated with removal efficiency at 96.3 %, 93.2 %, 94.9 %, and 96.4 % (Fig. 6e and f). Further, with the co-existence of metal ions (i.e., Cd2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, or Ba2+, 5 mM), the removal efficiency of CDs to Al3+ remains above 93 %. Thus, vine tea-derived CDs can be used as an excellent adsorbent for Al3+ removal from wastewater samples.

In conclusion, novel dual-functional CDs (combining detection with removal functions for Al3+) with AIE characteristics have been successfully synthesized by one-pot solvothermal treatment of vine tea. Enhanced fluorescence was found in aggregated state, at high concentrations, and in high viscosity solution, ascribing to the restricted intramolecular motion. By systematic profiling the response mechanism, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs were two active sites for Al3+ selective coordination with CDs to aggregates. Al3+ complexing with CDs resulted in CHEF and AIE phenomena for reliable, selective, sensitive, and fast quantitative sensing Al3+ in a fluorescence turn-on mode. Additionally, the strong binding affinity of Al3+ to CDs could assemble into CDs-Al3+ flocs for Al3+ removal with high adsorption capacity in a simple, efficient, and eco-friendly way, demonstrating the promising applications. Therefore, the current work not only elucidates the structure–property relationship of vine tea-derived CDs but also demonstrates the great potential of biomass-derived CDs with AIE property in sensing and removal of Al3+ for environmental monitoring and remediation.
